subject: Physics
q: Three charges $-q_1,+q_2$ and $-q_3$ are placed as shown in the figure. The $x$-component of the force on $-q_1$ is proportional to
question: Three charges $-q_1,+q_2$ and $-q_3$ are placed as shown in the figure. The $x$-component of the force on $-q_1$ is proportional to
solution:
The force body diagram $F_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_3}{a^2}$ $\mathrm{F}_2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{q}_1 \mathrm{q}_3}{\mathrm{~b}^2}$
$\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{X}}=\mathrm{F}_1 \sin \theta+\mathrm{F}_2=\frac{\mathrm{q}_1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{\mathrm{q}_3}{\mathrm{a}^2} \sin \theta+\frac{\mathrm{q}_1}{\mathrm{~b}_2}\right] \Rightarrow \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{X}} \propto\left(\frac{\mathrm{q}_3}{\mathrm{a}^2} \sin \theta+\frac{\mathrm{q}_2}{\mathrm{~b}^2}\right)$

subject: Physics
q: A point particle of charge $Q$ is located at $P$ along the axis of an electric dipole 1 at a distance $r$ as shown in the figure. The point P is also on the equatorial plane of a second electric dipole 2 at a distance r . The dipoles are made of opposite charge q separated by a distance $2 a$. For the charge particle at P not to experience any net force, which of the following correctly describes the situation?
question: A point particle of charge $Q$ is located at $P$ along the axis of an electric dipole 1 at a distance $r$ as shown in the figure. The point P is also on the equatorial plane of a second electric dipole 2 at a distance r . The dipoles are made of opposite charge q separated by a distance $2 a$. For the charge particle at P not to experience any net force, which of the following correctly describes the situation?
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{(r-a)^2}-\frac{1}{(r+a)^2}=\frac{2 a}{\left(a^2+r^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \\ & \frac{4 a r}{\left(r^2-a^2\right)^2}=\frac{2 a}{\left(a^2+r^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \\ & \left(r^2-a^2\right)^2=2 r\left(a^2+r^2\right)^{3 / 2} \\ & \left(1-\frac{a^2}{r^2}\right)^2=2\left(1+\frac{a^2}{r^2}\right)^{3 / 2} \\ & \left(1-x^2\right)^2=2\left(1+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}\left(x=\frac{a}{r}\right) \\ & \frac{\left(1-x^2\right)^2}{\left(1+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}}=2 \end{aligned}$ Now for $\mathrm{X}=3$ We get $\frac{64}{10 \sqrt{10}} \approx 2 \Rightarrow \frac{a}{r} \approx 3$ [But for $a > r$ point charge will between the dipole where $\vec{E} \neq 0$]

The electric flux through a surface area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> lying in YZ-plane(in SI unit) is:
question: An electric field is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>6</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The electric flux through a surface area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> lying in YZ-plane(in SI unit) is:
solution:
Given:

E→=(6i^+5j^+3k^) N C-1

A→=30i^ m2

ϕ=E→·A→

⇒ϕ=(6i^+5j^+3k^)·(30i^)=180 SI unit

The ratio of flux of reported field through the rectangular surface of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> (parallel to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi></math> plane) to that of the surface of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> (parallel to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi></math> plane) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>?</mo></math> [Here <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> are unit vectors along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> -axes respectively]
question: The electric field in a region is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The ratio of flux of reported field through the rectangular surface of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> (parallel to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi></math> plane) to that of the surface of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> (parallel to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi></math> plane) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>?</mo></math> [Here <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> are unit vectors along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> -axes respectively]
solution:
E→=3E05i^+4E05j^ N C-1

A1=0.2 m2 [parallel to y-z plane]

=A→1=0.2 m2i^

A2=0.3 m2 [parallel to x-z plane]

A→2=0.3 m2j^

Now, ϕa=3E05i^+4E05j^·0.2i^=3×0.25E0

& ϕb=3E05i^+4E05j^·0.3j^=4×0.35E0

Now, ϕaϕb=0.61.2=12=ab

⇒a:b=1:2

⇒a=1

subject: Physics
q: Charges -q and +q, located at A and B, respectively, constitute an electric dipole. Distance AB=2a, O is the mid point of the dipole and OP is perpendicular to AB. A charge Q is placed at P where OP=y and y≫2a. The charge Q experiences an electrostatic force F. If Q is now moved along the equatorial line to P' such that OP'=y3 the force on Q will be close toy3≪2a
question: Charges -q and +q, located at A and B, respectively, constitute an electric dipole. Distance AB=2a, O is the mid point of the dipole and OP is perpendicular to AB. A charge Q is placed at P where OP=y and y≫2a. The charge Q experiences an electrostatic force F. If Q is now moved along the equatorial line to P' such that OP'=y3 the force on Q will be close toy3≪2a
solution:
Electric field intensity at the distance y along the perpendicular bisector is

E1=KPy3

So, force at distance y on Q,

F=Q(E1)=QKPy3 ...(1)

F'=QKPy33 ...(2)

Dividing 1 by 2, we get,

F'F=33

∴ F'=27F

subject: Physics
q: Two charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>q</mi></math> are separated by a distance ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>’ in air. At a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> from charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math>, the resultant electric field is zero. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is :
question: Two charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>q</mi></math> are separated by a distance ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>’ in air. At a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> from charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math>, the resultant electric field is zero. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is :
solution:
Given: E→netP=0

⇒kqx2=k×3qr-x2

⇒r-x2=3x2

⇒r-x=3x

⇒x=r3+1

subject: Physics
q:
In free space, a particle A of charge 1 μC is held fixed at point P . Another particle B of the same charge and mass 4 μg is kept at a distance of 1 mm from P. If B is released, then its velocity at a distance of 9 mm from P is:
[Take  14πϵ0=9×109 N m2 C-2 ]
question:
In free space, a particle A of charge 1 μC is held fixed at point P . Another particle B of the same charge and mass 4 μg is kept at a distance of 1 mm from P. If B is released, then its velocity at a distance of 9 mm from P is:
[Take  14πϵ0=9×109 N m2 C-2 ]
solution:
From conservation in mechanical energy:
-ΔP.E.=ΔK.E.
14πε01×1062110-3-19×10-3=12×4×10-6×V2
9×109×10-1289×10-3=2×10-6×V2
V2=4×106
V=2×103 m s-1

subject: Physics
q:
The volume charge density of a sphere of radius 6 m is 2 μC cm-3. The number of lines of force per unit surface area coming out from the surface of the sphere is _____ ×1010 N C-1.

[Given : Permittivity of vacuum ϵ0=8.85×10-12C2 N-1-m-2]
question:
The volume charge density of a sphere of radius 6 m is 2 μC cm-3. The number of lines of force per unit surface area coming out from the surface of the sphere is _____ ×1010 N C-1.

[Given : Permittivity of vacuum ϵ0=8.85×10-12C2 N-1-m-2]
solution:
Given here, ρ=2 μC cm-3 and R=6 m.

Number of lines of force per unit area = electric field at surface=KQR2

E=14πε0ρ43πR3R2 

E=ρR3ϵ0=2×63×8.85×10-12=0.45×1012 N C-1

=45×1010 N C-1

q: In an electrical circuit, a battery is connected to pass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> of charge through it in a certain given time. The potential difference between two plates of the battery is maintained at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The workdone by the battery is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>
question: In an electrical circuit, a battery is connected to pass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> of charge through it in a certain given time. The potential difference between two plates of the battery is maintained at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The workdone by the battery is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>
solution:
Work done by battery =QΔV

⇒20×15=300 J

=300

q: Two isolated conducting spheres <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi></math> have <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi>C</mi></math> charges, respectively, and are at a large distance from each other, They are now connected by a conducting wire. A long time after this is done the charges on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are respectively:
question: Two isolated conducting spheres <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi></math> have <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi>C</mi></math> charges, respectively, and are at a large distance from each other, They are now connected by a conducting wire. A long time after this is done the charges on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are respectively:
solution:
Total charge =12-3=9 μC

If final charges are q1 and q2

q1q2=R1R2=21

q1=6 μC

q2=3 μC

subject: Physics
q: Two large plane parallel conducting plates are kept 10 cm apart as shown in figure. The potential difference between them is $V$. The potential difference between the points A and B (shown in the figure) is :
question: Two large plane parallel conducting plates are kept 10 cm apart as shown in figure. The potential difference between them is $V$. The potential difference between the points A and B (shown in the figure) is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Using } \Delta V=E(\Delta d) \\ & V=E(10) \\ & V_{A B}=E \cdot 4=\frac{V}{10} \times 4=\frac{2 V}{5}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: On moving a charge of 20 coulombs by $2 \mathrm{~cm}, 2 \mathrm{~J}$ of work is done, then the potential difference between the points is
question: On moving a charge of 20 coulombs by $2 \mathrm{~cm}, 2 \mathrm{~J}$ of work is done, then the potential difference between the points is
solution:
$$
\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{qV} \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=2 / 20=0.1 \mathrm{~V}
$$
Here $W$ is the work done in moving charge $q$ from point $A$ to $B$

subject: Physics
q: A point charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is placed at the origin. At what location on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis should a point charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> be placed so that the net electric field is zero at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis ?
question: A point charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is placed at the origin. At what location on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis should a point charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> be placed so that the net electric field is zero at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis ?
solution:
Let the charge 40 μC be placed at a distance X0 from the origin as shown in the figure.

For the electric field at x=2 to be 0,

EP=0⇒k×1022=k×40 X0-22

⇒12=2X0-2

⇒X0-2=4

Therefore, the charge is located at X0=4+2=6 cm

subject: Physics
q: Two point charges $+8 q$ and $-2 q$ are located at $x=0$ and $x=L$ respectively. The location of a point on the $x$ axis at which the net electric field due to these two point charges is zero is
question: Two point charges $+8 q$ and $-2 q$ are located at $x=0$ and $x=L$ respectively. The location of a point on the $x$ axis at which the net electric field due to these two point charges is zero is
solution:
$-\frac{\mathrm{k} 2 q}{(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{L})^2}+\frac{\mathrm{k} 8 \mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{x}^2}=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=2 \mathrm{~L}$

subject: Physics
q: An infinitely long positively charged straight thread has a linear charge density $\lambda \mathrm{Cm}^{-1}$. An electron revolves along a circular path having axis along the length of the wire. The graph that correctly represents the variation of the kinetic energy of electron as a function of radius of circular path from the wire is :
question: An infinitely long positively charged straight thread has a linear charge density $\lambda \mathrm{Cm}^{-1}$. An electron revolves along a circular path having axis along the length of the wire. The graph that correctly represents the variation of the kinetic energy of electron as a function of radius of circular path from the wire is :
solution:
Electric field $\mathrm{E}$ at a distance $\mathrm{r}$ due to infinite long wire is $E=\frac{2 k \lambda}{r}$ Force of electron $\Rightarrow F=e E$ $\begin{aligned} & F=e\left(\frac{2 k \lambda}{r}\right) \\ & F=\frac{2 k \lambda e}{r} \end{aligned}$
This force will provide required centripetal force $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{F} & =\frac{\mathrm{m}^2}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{k} \lambda \mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{r}} \\ \mathrm{v} & =\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{k} \lambda \mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{m}}} \\ \mathrm{KE} & =\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{k} \lambda \mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{m}}\right) \\ & =\mathrm{k} \lambda \mathrm{e} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Three infinitely long charged thin sheets are placed as shown in figure. The magnitude of electric field at the point $P$ is $\frac{x \sigma}{\epsilon_o}$. The value of $x$ is _______ (all quantities are measured in SI units).
question: Three infinitely long charged thin sheets are placed as shown in figure. The magnitude of electric field at the point $P$ is $\frac{x \sigma}{\epsilon_o}$. The value of $x$ is _______ (all quantities are measured in SI units).
solution: $\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{\mathrm{p}} & =\left(\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}+\frac{2 \sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}+\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}\right)(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}) \\ & =-\frac{2 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0} \hat{\mathrm{i}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Two identical charged spheres are suspended by strings of equal lengths. The strings make an angle θ with each other. When suspended in water the angle remains the same. If density of the material of the sphere is 1.5 g/cc, the dielectric constant of water will be ______.

(Take density of water =1 g/cc)
question:
Two identical charged spheres are suspended by strings of equal lengths. The strings make an angle θ with each other. When suspended in water the angle remains the same. If density of the material of the sphere is 1.5 g/cc, the dielectric constant of water will be ______.

(Take density of water =1 g/cc)
solution:
In air:

 tanθ2=Fmg=q24πε0r2mg

In water:

 tanθ2=F'mg'=q24πε0εrr2mgeff

Equate both equations, we get

ε0 g=ε0εrg1-11.5

⇒εr=3

subject: Physics
q: Two infinite identical charged sheets and a charged spherical body of charge density ' $\rho$ ' are arranged as shown in figure. Then the correct relation between the electrical fields at $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D points is :
question: Two infinite identical charged sheets and a charged spherical body of charge density ' $\rho$ ' are arranged as shown in figure. Then the correct relation between the electrical fields at $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D points is :
solution:
Conceptual
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{C}} \neq \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{D}} \\ & \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}} \gt \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two insulating plates are both uniformly charged in such a way that the potential difference between them is $V_2-V_1=20 \mathrm{~V}$. (i.e. plate 2 is at a higher potential). The plates are separated by $d=0.1 \mathrm{~m}$ and can be treated as infinitely large. An electron is released from rest on the inner surface of plate 1 . What is its speed when it hits plate $2 ?$ $\left(\mathrm{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}=9.11 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}\right)$
question: Two insulating plates are both uniformly charged in such a way that the potential difference between them is $V_2-V_1=20 \mathrm{~V}$. (i.e. plate 2 is at a higher potential). The plates are separated by $d=0.1 \mathrm{~m}$ and can be treated as infinitely large. An electron is released from rest on the inner surface of plate 1 . What is its speed when it hits plate $2 ?$ $\left(\mathrm{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}=9.11 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}\right)$
solution:
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2=\mathrm{eV}$
$\mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{eV}}{\mathrm{m}}}=2.65 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

subject: Physics
q: If a charge q is placed at the centre of a closed hemispherical non-conducting surface, the total flux passing through the flat surface would be
question: If a charge q is placed at the centre of a closed hemispherical non-conducting surface, the total flux passing through the flat surface would be
solution:
Assume a complete sphere, then flux due to a charge placed at the center of sphere is ϕ=qε0

Flux through hemisphere surface is equal to flux passing through flat surface ϕ=12×qε0=q2ε0

subject: Physics
q:
Two point dipoles of dipole moment $\vec{p}_1$ and $\vec{p}_2$ are at a distance $x$ from each other and $\vec{p}_1 || \vec{p}_2$. 
 The force between the dipoles is :
question:
Two point dipoles of dipole moment $\vec{p}_1$ and $\vec{p}_2$ are at a distance $x$ from each other and $\vec{p}_1 || \vec{p}_2$. 
 The force between the dipoles is :
solution: Force of interaction $\mathrm{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{3 \mathrm{p}_1 \mathrm{p}_2}{\mathrm{r}^4}$

subject: Physics
q:
A small bob of mass 100 mg and charge $+10 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is connected to an insulating string of length 1 m. It is brought near to an infinitely long nonconducting sheet of charge density ' $\sigma$ ' as shown in figure. If string subtends an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the sheet at equilibrium the charge density of sheet will be :
(Given, $\varepsilon_0=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \frac{\mathrm{~F}}{\mathrm{~m}}$ and acceleration due to gravity, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$)
question:
A small bob of mass 100 mg and charge $+10 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is connected to an insulating string of length 1 m. It is brought near to an infinitely long nonconducting sheet of charge density ' $\sigma$ ' as shown in figure. If string subtends an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the sheet at equilibrium the charge density of sheet will be :
(Given, $\varepsilon_0=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \frac{\mathrm{~F}}{\mathrm{~m}}$ and acceleration due to gravity, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{qE}=\mathrm{mg} \\ & \mathrm{q}\left[\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}\right]=\mathrm{mg} \\ & \sigma=\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{q}} \\ & \sigma=\frac{2 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 10}{10 \times 10^{-6}} \\ & \sigma=17.7 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}^2 \\ & \sigma=1.77 \mathrm{nC} / \mathrm{m}^2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: An electric field $\vec{E}=(2 x \hat{i}) N C^{-1}$ exists in space. A cube of side $2 \mathrm{~m}$ is placed in the space as per figure given below. The electric flux through the cube is ______ $\mathrm{Nm}^2 / \mathrm{C}$.
question: An electric field $\vec{E}=(2 x \hat{i}) N C^{-1}$ exists in space. A cube of side $2 \mathrm{~m}$ is placed in the space as per figure given below. The electric flux through the cube is ______ $\mathrm{Nm}^2 / \mathrm{C}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=2 x \hat{\mathrm{i}} \\ & \phi=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \phi_{\text {in }}=-4 \times 4=-16 \mathrm{Nm}^2 / \mathrm{c} \\ & \phi_{\text {out }}=8 \times 4=32 \mathrm{Nm}^2 / \mathrm{c} \\ & d_{\text {net }}=\phi_{\text {in }+} \phi_{\text {out }}=-16+32=16 \mathrm{Nm}^2 / \mathrm{c}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Electric charge is transferred to an irregular metallic disk as shown in figure. If $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ and $\sigma_4$ are charge densities at given points then, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question: Electric charge is transferred to an irregular metallic disk as shown in figure. If $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ and $\sigma_4$ are charge densities at given points then, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \sigma \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{ROC}} \\ & (\mathrm{ROC})_1 \lt (\mathrm{ROC})_3 \lt (\mathrm{ROC})_2=(\mathrm{ROC})_4 \\ & \sigma_1 \gt \sigma_3 \gt \sigma_2=\sigma_4\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A cubical volume is bounded by the surfaces x=0, x=a, y=0, y=a, z=0, z=a. The electric field in the region is given by E→=E0xi^. Where E0=4×104 NC-1 m-1. If a=2 cm, the charge contained in the cubical volume is Q×10–14 C. The value of Q is ______.

(Take ϵ0=9×10-12 C2 N-1m-2)
question:
A cubical volume is bounded by the surfaces x=0, x=a, y=0, y=a, z=0, z=a. The electric field in the region is given by E→=E0xi^. Where E0=4×104 NC-1 m-1. If a=2 cm, the charge contained in the cubical volume is Q×10–14 C. The value of Q is ______.

(Take ϵ0=9×10-12 C2 N-1m-2)
solution:
Given here, electric field, E→=E0xi^

Here, the flux passes mainly through surface areas, ABCD and EFGH. As the surfaces AEFB and CGHD are parallel to the electric field, so flux for these surfaces are zero. Again in EFGH, a=0, thus, electric field is zero.

Hence, the flux only passes through the surface are ABCD.

The net flux is ϕnet=ϕABCD=E0a·a2

Using Gauss's law, qenϵ0=E0a3

⇒qen=E0ε0a3

=4×104×9×10-12×8×10-6

=288×10-14 C

Hence, the value of Q=288.

subject: Physics
q: Two shorts dipoles $(A, B), A$ having charges $\pm 2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and length 1 cm and $B$ having charges $\pm 4 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and length 1 cm are placed with their centres 80 cm apart as shown in the figure. The electric field at a point $P$, equi-distant from the centres of both dipoles is $\_\_\_\_$ N/C.
question: Two shorts dipoles $(A, B), A$ having charges $\pm 2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and length 1 cm and $B$ having charges $\pm 4 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and length 1 cm are placed with their centres 80 cm apart as shown in the figure. The electric field at a point $P$, equi-distant from the centres of both dipoles is $\_\_\_\_$ N/C.
solution:
$\vec{E}_2 = -\dfrac{KP_2}{r^3}$ ; $\vec{E}_1 = -\dfrac{2KP_1}{r^3}$
$P_1 = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-2} = 2 \times 10^{-8}$
$P_2 = 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-2} = 4 \times 10^{-8}$
$\vec{E}_{net} = \dfrac{2 \times 9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 10^{-8}}{(0.4)^3} \hat{i} - \dfrac{9 \times 10^9 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}{(0.4)^3} \hat{j}$
$\vec{E}_{net} = \dfrac{9 \times 10^9 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}{(0.4)^3} [\hat{i} - \hat{j}]$
$|\vec{E}_{net}| = \dfrac{9 \times 10^4}{16} (\sqrt{2})$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements
Statement I : An electric dipole is placed at the centre of a hollow sphere. The flux of electric field through the sphere is zero, but the electric field is not zero anywhere in the sphere.

Statement II : If R is the radius of a solid metallic sphere and Q be the total charge on it. The electric field at any point on the spherical surface of radius r<R is zero but the electric flux passing through this closed spherical surface of radius r is not

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements
Statement I : An electric dipole is placed at the centre of a hollow sphere. The flux of electric field through the sphere is zero, but the electric field is not zero anywhere in the sphere.

Statement II : If R is the radius of a solid metallic sphere and Q be the total charge on it. The electric field at any point on the spherical surface of radius r<R is zero but the electric flux passing through this closed spherical surface of radius r is not

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are true
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is false but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is false
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are false
solution:
∮E→·ds→=qinε0=0=ϕ

Flux of E→ through sphere is zero.

But ∮E→·ds→=0⇒E→·ds→≠0 for small section ds only

Statement-2

As change encloses within gaussian surface is equal to zero.

ϕ=∮E→·ds→=0

subject: Physics
q: For a uniformly charged thin spherical shell, the electric potential V radially away from the centre O of shell can be graphically represented as
question: For a uniformly charged thin spherical shell, the electric potential V radially away from the centre O of shell can be graphically represented as
solution:
The potential is constant inside the sphere as the electric field is zero inside the sphere. So, the value is given by 

V=Q4πε0R for r<R

The potential at the surface is 

V=Q4πε0R

The potential outside the sphere starts decreasing and is given by the formula 

V=Q4πε0r for r>R

subject: Physics
q: An electric field, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{6}}$ passes through the surface of $4 \mathrm{~m}^2$ area having unit vector $\hat{n}=\left(\frac{2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{6}}\right)$. The electric flux for that surface is ______ $\mathrm{Vm}$.
question: An electric field, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{6}}$ passes through the surface of $4 \mathrm{~m}^2$ area having unit vector $\hat{n}=\left(\frac{2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{6}}\right)$. The electric flux for that surface is ______ $\mathrm{Vm}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} \phi & =\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}} \\ & =\left(\frac{2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+8 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{6}}\right) \cdot 4\left(\frac{2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{6}}\right) \\ & =\frac{4}{6} \times(4+6+8)=12 \mathrm{Vm}\end{aligned}$
